Note that we specify the version for the transformers package as a temporary fix to a recent issue:\nhttps://github.com/huggingface/trl/issues/2338\n>\nuv\npip\ninstall\n-U\ntorch\ntransformers\n==\n4\n.48.3\naccelerate\ntrl\npeft\nWe recommend using\nuv\npackage manager to speed up the installation.\nRecommended: Set up MLflow Tracing to understand what\'s happening under the hood.\nMLflow DSPy Integration\n¶\nMLflow\nis an LLMOps tool that natively integrates with DSPy and offer explainability and experiment tracking. In this tutorial, you can use MLflow to visualize prompts and optimization progress as traces to understand the DSPy\'s behavior better. You can set up MLflow easily by following the four steps below.\nInstall MLflow\n%pip\ninstall\nmlflow>\n=\n2\n.20\nStart MLflow UI in a separate terminal\nmlflow\nui\n--port\n5000\nConnect the notebook to MLflow\nimport\nmlflow\nmlflow\n.\nset_tracking_uri\n(\n"http://localhost:5000"\n)\nmlflow\n.\nset_experiment\n(\n"DSPy"\n)\nEnabling tracing.\nmlflow\n.\ndspy\n.\nautolog\n()\nTo learn more about the integration, visit\nMLflow DSPy Documentation\nas well.\nDataset\n¶\nFor this tutorial, we will use the Banking77 dataset.\nIn\xa0[1]:\nCopied!\nimport\ndspy\nimport\nrandom\nfrom\ndspy.datasets\nimport\nDataLoader\nfrom\ndatasets\nimport\nload_dataset\n# Load the Banking77 dataset.\nCLASSES\n=\nload_dataset\n(\n"PolyAI/banking77"\n,\nsplit\n=\n"train"\n,\ntrust_remote_code\n=\nTrue\n)\n.\nfeatures\n[\n\'label\'\n]\n.\nnames\nkwargs\n=\ndict\n(\nfields\n=\n(\n"text"\n,\n"label"\n),\ninput_keys\n=\n(\n"text"\n,),\nsplit\n=\n"train"\n,\ntrust_remote_code\n=\nTrue\n)\n# Load the first 2000 examples from the dataset, and assign a hint to each *training* example.\nraw_data\n=\n[\ndspy\n.\nExample\n(\nx\n,\nlabel\n=\nCLASSES\n[\nx\n.\nlabel\n])\n.\nwith_inputs\n(\n"text"\n)\nfor\nx\nin\nDataLoader\n()\n.\nfrom_huggingface\n(\ndataset_name\n=\n"PolyAI/banking77"\n,\n**\nkwargs\n)[:\n1000\n]\n]\nrandom\n.\nRandom\n(\n0\n)\n.\nshuffle\n(\nraw_data\n)\nimport dspy\nimport random\nfrom dspy.datasets import DataLoader\nfrom datasets import load_dataset\n\n# Load the Banking77 dataset.\nCLASSES = load_dataset("PolyAI/banking77", split="train", trust_remote_code=True).features[\'label\'].names\nkwargs = dict(fields=("text", "label"), input_keys=("text",), split="train", trust_remote_code=True)\n\n# Load the first 2000 examples from the dataset, and assign a hint to each *training* example.\nraw_data = [\n    dspy.Example(x, label=CLASSES[x.label]).with_inputs("text")\n    for x in DataLoader().from_huggingface(dataset_name="PolyAI/banking77", **kwargs)[:1000]\n]\n\nrandom.Random(0).shuffle(raw_data)\nThis dataset has 77 different categories for classification. Let\'s review some of them.\nIn\xa0[2]:\nCopied!\nlen\n(\nCLASSES\n),\nCLASSES\n[:\n10\n]\nlen(CLASSES), CLASSES[:10]\nOut[2]:\n(77,\n [\'activate_my_card\',\n  \'age_limit\',\n  \'apple_pay_or_google_pay\',\n  \'atm_support\',\n  \'automatic_top_up\',\n  \'balance_not_updated_after_bank_transfer\',\n  \'balance_not_updated_after_cheque_or_cash_deposit\',\n  \'beneficiary_not_allowed\',\n  \'cancel_transfer\',\n  \'card_about_to_expire\'])\nLet us sample 500 (unlabeled) queries from Banking77. We\'ll use these for our bootstrapped finetuning.\nIn\xa0[3]:\nCopied!\nunlabeled_trainset\n=\n[\ndspy\n.\nExample\n(\ntext\n=\nx\n.\ntext\n)\n.\nwith_inputs\n(\n"text"\n)\nfor\nx\nin\nraw_data\n[:\n500\n]]\nunlabeled_trainset\n[\n0\n]\nunlabeled_trainset = [dspy.Example(text=x.text).with_inputs("text") for x in raw_data[:500]]\n\nunlabeled_trainset[0]\nOut[3]:\nExample({\'text\': \'What if there is an error on the exchange rate?\'}) (input_keys={\'text\'})\nDSPy program\n¶\nLet\'s say that we want a program that takes the\ntext\nand reasons step by step and then selects one of the classes from Banking77.\nNote that this is meant mainly for illustration, or for cases where you want to inspect the model\'s reasoning, e.g. for a small degree of explainability. In other words, this type of task is not necessarily likely to benefit very much from explicit reasoning.\nIn\xa0[4]:\nCopied!\nfrom\ntyping\nimport\nLiteral\nclassify\n=\ndspy\n.\nChainOfThought\n(\nf\n"text -> label: Literal\n{\nCLASSES\n}\n"\n)\nfrom typing import Literal\n\nclassify = dspy.ChainOfThought(f"text -> label: Literal{CLASSES}")\nBootstrapped finetuning\n¶\nThere are many ways to go about this, e.g. allowing the model to teach itself or using inference-time compute (e.g., ensembling) to identify cases of high confidence without labels.\nPerhaps the simplest is to use a model that we\'d expect can do a reasonable job at this task as a teacher of reasoning and classification, and to distill that to our small model. All of these patterns can be expressed in a handful of lines of code.\nLet\'s set up the tiny\nLlama-3.2-1B-Instruct\nas a student LM. We\'ll use GPT-4o-mini as a teacher LM.\nIn\xa0[5]:\nCopied!\nfrom\ndspy.clients.lm_local\nimport\nLocalProvider\nstudent_lm_name\n=\n"meta-llama/Llama-3.2-1B-Instruct"\nstudent_lm\n=\ndspy\n.\nLM\n(\nmodel\n=\nf\n"openai/local:\n{\nstudent_lm_name\n}\n"\n,\nprovider\n=\nLocalProvider\n(),\nmax_tokens\n=\n2000\n)\nteacher_lm\n=\ndspy\n.\nLM\n(\n\'openai/gpt-4o-mini\'\n,\nmax_tokens\n=\n3000\n)\nfrom dspy.clients.lm_local import LocalProvider\n\nstudent_lm_name = "meta-llama/Llama-3.2-1B-Instruct"\nstudent_lm = dspy.LM(model=f"openai/local:{student_lm_name}", provider=LocalProvider(), max_tokens=2000)\nteacher_lm = dspy.LM(\'openai/gpt-4o-mini\', max_tokens=3000)\nNow, let\'s assign classifiers to our LMs.\nIn\xa0[6]:\nCopied!\nstudent_classify\n=\nclassify\n.\ndeepcopy\n()\nstudent_classify\n.\nset_lm\n(\nstudent_lm\n)\nteacher_classify\n=\nclassify\n.\ndeepcopy\n()\nteacher_classify\n.\nset_lm\n(\nteacher_lm\n)\nstudent_classify = classify.deepcopy()\nstudent_classify.set_lm(student_lm)\n\nteacher_classify = classify.deepcopy()\nteacher_classify.set_lm(teacher_lm)\nLet\'s now launch the bootstrapped finetuning. The word "bootstrapped" here means that the program itself will be invoked on the training inputs and the resulting traces seen over all modules will be recorded and used for finetuning. This is the weight-optimizing variant of the various BootstrapFewShot methods in DSPy.\nOn every question in the (unlabeled) training set, this will invoke the teacher program, which will produce reasoning and select a class. This will be traced and then constitute a training set for all modules (in this case, just the one CoT module) in the student program.\nWhen the\ncompile\nmethod is called, the\nBootstrapFinetune\noptimizer will use the passed teacher program (or programs, you can pass a list!) to create a training dataset.\nIt will then use this training dataset to create a fine-tuned version of the LM set for the\nstudent\nprogram, replacing it with the trained LM.\nNote that the trained LM will be a new LM instance (the\nstudent_lm\nobject we instantiated here will be untouched!)\nNote: If you have labels, you can pass\nmetric\nto the constructor of\nBootstrapFinetune\n. If you want to apply this in practice, you can pass\ntrain_kwargs\nto the constructor to control local LM training settings:\ndevice\n,\nuse_peft\n,\nnum_train_epochs\n,\nper_device_train_batch_size\n,\ngradient_accumulation_steps\n,\nlearning_rate\n,\nmax_seq_length\n,\npacking\n,\nbf16\n, and\noutput_dir\n.\nIn\xa0[\xa0]:\nCopied!\n# Optional:\n# [1] You can set `DSPY_FINETUNEDIR` environment variable to control where the directory that will be used to store the\n#     checkpoints and fine-tuning data. If this is not set, `DSPY_CACHEDIR` is used by default.\n# [2] You can set the `CUDA_VISIBLE_DEVICES` environment variable to control the GPU that will be used for fine-tuning\n#     and inference. If this is not set and the default GPU that\'s used by HuggingFace\'s `transformers` library is\n#     occupied, an OutOfMemoryError might be raised.\n#\n# import os\n# os.environ["CUDA_VISIBLE_DEVICES"] = "0"\n# os.environ["DSPY_FINETUNEDIR"] = "/path/to/dir"\n# Optional:\n# [1] You can set `DSPY_FINETUNEDIR` environment variable to control where the directory that will be used to store the\n#     checkpoints and fine-tuning data. If this is not set, `DSPY_CACHEDIR` is used by default.\n# [2] You can set the `CUDA_VISIBLE_DEVICES` environment variable to control the GPU that will be used for fine-tuning\n#     and inference. If this is not set and the default GPU that\'s used by HuggingFace\'s `transformers` library is\n#     occupied, an OutOfMemoryError might be raised.\n#\n# import os\n# os.environ["CUDA_VISIBLE_DEVICES"] = "0"\n# os.environ["DSPY_FINETUNEDIR"] = "/path/to/dir"\nIn\xa0[7]:\nCopied!\ndspy\n.\nsettings\n.\nexperimental\n=\nTrue\n# fine-tuning is an experimental feature, so we set a flag to enable it\noptimizer\n=\ndspy\n.\nBootstrapFinetune\n(\nnum_threads\n=\n16\n)\n# if you *do* have labels, pass metric=your_metric here!\nclassify_ft\n=\noptimizer\n.\ncompile\n(\nstudent_classify\n,\nteacher\n=\nteacher_classify\n,\ntrainset\n=\nunlabeled_trainset\n)\ndspy.settings.experimental = True  # fine-tuning is an experimental feature, so we set a flag to enable it\n\noptimizer = dspy.BootstrapFinetune(num_threads=16)  # if you *do* have labels, pass metric=your_metric here!\nclassify_ft = optimizer.compile(student_classify, teacher=teacher_classify, trainset=unlabeled_trainset)\nSince this is a local model, we need to explicitly launch it.\nIn\xa0[8]:\nCopied!\nclassify_ft\n.\nget_lm\n()\n.\nlaunch\n()\nclassify_ft.get_lm().launch()\nValidating the finetuned program\n¶\nLet\'s now figure out if this was successful. We can ask the system one question and inspect its behavior.\nIn\xa0[9]:\nCopied!\nclassify_ft\n(\ntext\n=\n"I didn\'t receive my money earlier and it says the transaction is still in progress. Can you fix it?"\n)\nclassify_ft(text="I didn\'t receive my money earlier and it says the transaction is still in progress. Can you fix it?")\nOut[9]:\nPrediction(\n    reasoning=\'The user is inquiring about a specific issue, which they did not receive and is still showing as a pending transaction. This situation typically indicates a problem with the cash withdrawal process, as the user is not receiving the money they attempted to withdraw. The appropriate label for this scenario is "pending_cash_withdrawal," as it directly relates to the status of the cash withdrawal transaction.\',\n    label=\'pending_cash_withdrawal\'\n)\nWe could also get a small set of gold labels and see if the system can generalize to unseen queries.\nIn\xa0[10]:\nCopied!\ndevset\n=\nraw_data\n[\n500\n:\n600\n]\ndevset\n[\n0\n]\ndevset = raw_data[500:600]\ndevset[0]\nOut[10]:\nExample({\'text\': \'Which fiat currencies do you currently support? Will this change in this future?\', \'label\': \'fiat_currency_support\'}) (input_keys={\'text\'})\nLet\'s define an evaluator on this small dev set, where the metric ignores the reasoning and checks that the label is exactly correct.\nIn\xa0[11]:\nCopied!\nmetric\n=\n(\nlambda\nx\n,\ny\n,\ntrace\n=\nNone\n:\nx\n.\nlabel\n==\ny\n.\nlabel\n)\nevaluate\n=\ndspy\n.\nEvaluate\n(\ndevset\n=\ndevset\n,\nmetric\n=\nmetric\n,\ndisplay_progress\n=\nTrue\n,\ndisplay_table\n=\n5\n,\nnum_threads\n=\n16\n)\nmetric = (lambda x, y, trace=None: x.label == y.label)\nevaluate = dspy.Evaluate(devset=devset, metric=metric, display_progress=True, display_table=5, num_threads=16)\nNow, let\'s evaluate the finetuned 1B classifier.\nIn\xa0[12]:\nCopied!\nevaluate\n(\nclassify_ft\n)\nevaluate(classify_ft)\nAverage Metric: 51.00 / 99 (51.5%): 100%|██████████| 100/100 [00:35<00:00,  2.79it/s]\ntext\nexample_label\nreasoning\npred_label\n<lambda>\nlabel\n0\nWhich fiat currencies do you currently support? Compared to simply saving it to a local file, MLflow offers the following benefits:\nDependency Management\n: MLflow automatically save the frozen environment metadata along with the program to ensure reproducibility.\nExperiment Tracking\n: With MLflow, you can track the program\'s performance and cost along with the program itself.\nCollaboration\n: You can share the program and results with your team members by sharing the MLflow experiment.\nTo save the program in MLflow, run the following code:\nimport\nmlflow\n# Start an MLflow Run and save the program\nwith\nmlflow\n.\nstart_run\n(\nrun_name\n=\n"optimized_classifier"\n):\nmodel_info\n=\nmlflow\n.\ndspy\n.\nlog_model\n(\nclassify_ft\n,\nartifact_path\n=\n"model"\n,\n# Any name to save the program in MLflow\n)\n# Load the program back from MLflow\nloaded\n=\nmlflow\n.\ndspy\n.\nload_model\n(\nmodel_info\n.\nmodel_uri\n)\nTo learn more about the integration, visit\nMLflow DSPy Documentation\nas well.', 'last_updated': '2025-04-11T17:41:09.725242'}

This uses\nasyncify under the hood and inherits the execution semantics.\ndspy_program\n=\ndspy\n.\nasyncify\n(\ndspy\n.\nChainOfThought\n(\n"question -> answer"\n))\nstreaming_dspy_program\n=\ndspy\n.\nstreamify\n(\ndspy_program\n)\n@app\n.\npost\n(\n"/predict/stream"\n)\nasync\ndef\nstream\n(\nquestion\n:\nQuestion\n):\nasync\ndef\ngenerate\n():\nasync\nfor\nvalue\nin\nstreaming_dspy_program\n(\nquestion\n=\nquestion\n.\ntext\n):\nif\nisinstance\n(\nvalue\n,\ndspy\n.\nPrediction\n):\ndata\n=\n{\n"prediction"\n:\nvalue\n.\nlabels\n()\n.\ntoDict\n()}\nelif\nisinstance\n(\nvalue\n,\nlitellm\n.\nModelResponse\n):\ndata\n=\n{\n"chunk"\n:\nvalue\n.\njson\n()}\nyield\nf\n"data:\n{\nujson\n.\ndumps\n(\ndata\n)\n}\n\\n\\n\n"\nyield\n"data: [DONE]\n\\n\\n\n"\nreturn\nStreamingResponse\n(\ngenerate\n(),\nmedia_type\n=\n"text/event-stream"\n)\n# Since you\'re often going to want to stream the result of a DSPy program as server-sent events,\n# we\'ve included a helper function for that, which is equivalent to the code above.\nfrom\ndspy.utils.streaming\nimport\nstreaming_response\n@app\n.\npost\n(\n"/predict/stream"\n)\nasync\ndef\nstream\n(\nquestion\n:\nQuestion\n):\nstream\n=\nstreaming_dspy_program\n(\nquestion\n=\nquestion\n.\ntext\n)\nreturn\nStreamingResponse\n(\nstreaming_response\n(\nstream\n),\nmedia_type\n=\n"text/event-stream"\n)\nWrite your code to a file, e.g.,\nfastapi_dspy.py\n. The program extracts entities referring to people from a list of input tokens using language model (LM) prompting.Use thedspy.LMclass anddspy.settings.configure()method to configure the language model that DSPy will use when invoking the program."}, {'level': 2, 'title': 'Define Metric and Evaluation Functions¶', 'content': "In DSPy, evaluating a program's performance is critical for iterative development. The optimization process allowed us to quickly improve performance without manually crafting prompts or tweaking parameters.\n\nNext Steps:\n\nExperiment with extraction of other entity types (e.g., locations or organizations).Explore DSPy's other builtin modules likeReActfor more complex reasoning tasks.Use the system in larger workflows, such as large scale document processing or summarization."}], 'full_text': 'Tutorial: Entity Extraction\n¶\nThis tutorial demonstrates how to perform\nentity extraction\nusing the CoNLL-2003 dataset with DSPy. entities referring to people from tokenized text.\nThen, we configure DSPy to use a particular language model (\ngpt-4o-mini\n) for all invocations of the program.\nKey DSPy Concepts Introduced:\nSignatures:\nDefine structured input/output schemas for your program.\nModules:\nEncapsulate program logic in reusable, composable units.\nSpecifically, we\'ll:\nCreate a\nPeopleExtraction\nDSPy Signature to specify the input (\ntokens\n) and output (\nextracted_people\n) fields.\nDefine a\npeople_extractor\nprogram that uses DSPy\'s The program extracts entities referring to people from a list of input tokens using language model (LM) prompting.\nUse the\ndspy.LM\nclass and\ndspy.settings.configure()\nmethod to configure the language model that DSPy will use when invoking the program.\nIn\xa0[3]:\nCopied!\nfrom\ntyping\nimport\nList\nclass\nPeopleExtraction\n(\ndspy\n.\nSignature\n):\n"""\nExtract contiguous tokens referring to specific people, if any, from a list of string tokens.\nOutput a list of tokens. In other words, do not combine multiple tokens into a single value.\n"""\ntokens\n:\nlist\n[\nstr\n]\n=\ndspy\n.\nInputField\n(\ndesc\n=\n"tokenized text"\n)\nextracted_people\n:\nlist\n[\nstr\n]\n=\ndspy\n.\nOutputField\n(\ndesc\n=\n"all tokens referring to specific people extracted from the tokenized text"\n)\npeople_extractor\n=\ndspy\n.\nChainOfThought\n(\nPeopleExtraction\n)\nfrom typing import List\n\nclass PeopleExtraction(dspy.Signature):\n    """\n    Extract contiguous tokens referring to specific people, if any, from a list of string tokens.\n    Output a list of tokens. In other words, do not combine multiple tokens into a single value.\n    """\n    tokens: list[str] = dspy.InputField(desc="tokenized text")\n    extracted_people: list[str] = dspy.OutputField(desc="all tokens referring to specific people extracted from the tokenized text")\n\npeople_extractor = dspy.ChainOfThought(PeopleExtraction)\nHere, we tell DSPy to use OpenAI\'s\ngpt-4o-mini\nmodel in our program. A good evaluation framework allows us to:\nMeasure the quality of our program\'s outputs.\nCompare outputs against ground-truth labels.\nIdentify areas for improvement.\nWhat We\'ll Do:\nDefine a custom metric (\nextraction_correctness_metric\n) to evaluate whether the extracted entities match the ground truth.\nCreate an evaluation function (\nevaluate_correctness\n) to apply this metric to a training or test dataset and compute the overall accuracy.\nThe evaluation function uses DSPy\'s\nEvaluate\nutility to handle parallelism and visualization of results.\nIn\xa0[5]:\nCopied!\ndef\nextraction_correctness_metric\n(\nexample\n:\ndspy\n.\nExample\n,\nprediction\n:\ndspy\n.\nPrediction\n,\ntrace\n=\nNone\n)\n->\nbool\n:\n"""\nComputes correctness program.\ntrace: Optional trace object for debugging.\nReturns:\nbool: True if predictions match expectations, False otherwise.\n"""\nreturn\nprediction\n.\nextracted_people\n==\nexample\n.\nexpected_extracted_people\nevaluate_correctness\n=\ndspy\n.\nEvaluate\n(\ndevset\n=\ntest_set\n,\nmetric\n=\nextraction_correctness_metric\n,\nnum_threads\n=\n24\n,\ndisplay_progress\n=\nTrue\n,\ndisplay_table\n=\nTrue\n)\ndef extraction_correctness_metric(example: dspy.Example, prediction: dspy.Prediction, trace=None) -> bool:\n    """\n    Computes correctness of entity extraction predictions.\n    \n    Args:\n trace object for debugging.\n    \n    Returns:\n        bool: True if predictions match expectations, False otherwise.\n    """\n    return prediction.extracted_people == example.expected_extracted_people\n\nevaluate_correctness = dspy.Evaluate(\n    devset=test_set,\n    metric=extraction_correctness_metric,\n    num_threads=24,\n    display_progress=True,\n MLflow, you can track the program\'s performance and cost along with the program itself.\nCollaboration\n: You can share the program and results with your team members by sharing the MLflow It responds simply with the next action.\nIn the\nforward\nmethod, we just initialize an environment for the given task\nidx\n. And we loop up to\nself.max_iters\n,\nrepeatedly invoking the\nself.react\nmodule to take the next action.\nIn\xa0[4]:\nCopied!\nclass\nAgent\n(\ndspy\n.\nModule\n):\ndef\n__init__\n(\nself\n,\nmax_iters\n=\n50\n,\nverbose\n=\nFalse\n):\nself\n.\nmax_iters\n=\nmax_iters\nself\n.\nverbose\n=\nverbose\nself\n.\nreact\n=\ndspy\n.\nPredict\n(\n"task, trajectory, possible_actions: list[str] -> action"\n)\ndef\nforward\n(\nself\n,\nidx\n):\nwith\nalfworld\n.\nPOOL\n.\nsession\n()\nas\nenv\n:\ntrajectory\n=\n[]\ntask\n,\ninfo\n=\nenv\n.\ninit\n(\nidx\n)\nif\nself\n.\nverbose\n:\nprint\n(\nf\n"Task:\n{\ntask\n}\n"\n)\nfor\n_\nin\nrange\n(\nself\n.\nmax_iters\n):\ntrajectory_\n=\n"\n\\n\n"\n.\njoin\n(\ntrajectory\n)\npossible_actions\n=\ninfo\n[\n"admissible_commands"\n][\n0\n]\n+\n[\n"think: ${...thoughts...}"\n]\nprediction\n=\nself\n.\nreact\n(\ntask\n=\ntask\n,\ntrajectory\n=\ntrajectory_\n,\npossible_actions\n=\npossible_actions\n)\ntrajectory\n.\nappend\n(\nf\n">\n{\nprediction\n.\naction\n}\n"\n)\nif\nprediction\n.\naction\n.\nstartswith\n(\n"think:"\n):\ntrajectory\n.\nappend\n(\n"OK."\n)\ncontinue\nobs\n,\nreward\n,\ndone\n,\ninfo\n=\nenv\n.\nstep\n(\nprediction\n.\naction\n)\nobs\n,\nreward\n,\ndone\n=\nobs\n[\n0\n],\nreward\n[\n0\n],\ndone\n[\n0\n]\ntrajectory\n.\nappend\n(\nobs\n)\nif\nself\n.\nverbose\n:\nprint\n(\n"\n\\n\n"\n.\njoin\n(\ntrajectory\n[\n-\n2\n:]))\nif\ndone\n:\nbreak\nassert\nreward\n==\nint\n(\ninfo\n[\n"won"\n][\n0\n]),\n(\nreward\n,\ninfo\n[\n"won"\n][\n0\n])\nreturn\ndspy\n.\nPrediction\n(\ntrajecotry\n=\ntrajectory\n,\nsuccess\n=\nreward\n)\nclass Agent(dspy.Module):\n    def __init__(self, max_iters=50, verbose=False):\n        self.max_iters = max_iters\n        self.verbose = verbose\n        self.react = dspy.Predict("task, trajectory, possible_actions: list[str] -> action")\n\n    def forward(self, idx):\n        with alfworld.POOL.session() as env:\n            trajectory = []\n            task, info = env.init(idx)\n            if self.verbose:\n                print(f"Task: {task}")\n\n            for _ in range(self.max_iters):\n                trajectory_ = "\\n".join(trajectory)\n                possible_actions = info["admissible_commands"][0] + ["think: ${...thoughts...}"]\n                prediction = self.react(task=task, trajectory=trajectory_, possible_actions=possible_actions)\n                trajectory.append(f"> {prediction.action}")\n\n                if prediction.action.startswith("think:"):\n                    trajectory.append("OK.")\n                    continue\n\n                obs, reward, done, info = env.step(prediction.action)\n                obs, reward, done = obs[0], reward[0], done[0]\n                trajectory.append(obs)\n\n                if self.verbose:\n                    print("\\n".join(trajectory[-2:]))\n\n                if done:\n                    break\n\n        assert reward == int(info["won"][0]), (reward, info["won"][0])\n        return dspy.Prediction(trajecotry=trajectory, success=reward)\nAside: If you wanted to include instructions for your agent...\n¶\nAbove, we opted to keep the agent super simple, without even providing short instructions that describe the task.\nIn principle, you can copy a short definition of the AlfWorld task (based on Yao et al., 2022) and use that as the\ninstruction for your agent. This is not inherently essential, but it helps illustrate the role that\ninstructions play in DSPy: they\'re not for coercing the model to exhibit a certain behavior, but they\'re there to\ndescribe the fundamentals of the task in a straightforward, human-readable way.\nIf you want to do that, you can simply replace this:\nself\n.\nreact\n=\ndspy\n.\nPredict\n(\n"task, trajectory, possible_actions: list[str] -> action"\n)\nwith this:\nINSTRUCTIONS\n=\n"""\nInteract with a simulated household to achieve a high-level goal. "fal-ai/flux-pro/v1.1-ultra",\n        arguments={\n            "prompt": prompt\n        },\n    ).request_id\n\n    result = fal_client.result("fal-ai/flux-pro/v1.1-ultra", request_id)\n    url = His weight at the beginning of the workouts is $244$ pounds. How many pounds does he expect to weigh at the end of the ten weeks? Express your answer to the nearest whole number.\nAnswer: 221\nNow let\'s define our module. It\'s extremely simple: just a chain-of-thought step that takes a\nquestion\nand produces an\nanswer\n.\nIn\xa0[4]:\nCopied!\nmodule\n=\ndspy\n.\nChainOfThought\n(\n"question -> answer"\n)\nmodule\n(\nquestion\n=\nexample\n.\nquestion\n)\nmodule = dspy.ChainOfThought("question -> answer")\nmodule(question=example.question)\nOut[4]:\nPrediction(\n    reasoning="Cal O\'Ree\'s weight loss each week is $1\\\\%$ of his weight at the end of the previous week. This means that at the end of each week, he retains $99\\\\%$ of his weight from the previous week. \\n\\nIf we denote his weight at the beginning as \\\\( W_0 = 244 \\\\) pounds, then his weight at the end of week \\\\( n \\\\) can be expressed as:\\n\\\\[\\nW_n = W_{n-1} \\\\times 0.99\\n\\\\]\\nThis can be simplified to:\\n\\\\[\\nW_n = W_0 \\\\times (0.99)^n\\n\\\\]\\nAfter 10 weeks, his weight will be:\\n\\\\[\\nW_{10} = 244 \\\\times (0.99)^{10}\\n\\\\]\\n\\nNow, we calculate \\\\( (0.99)^{10} \\\\):\\n\\\\[\\n(0.99)^{10} \\\\approx 0.904382\\n\\\\]\\n\\nNow, we can calculate his expected weight after 10 weeks:\\n\\\\[\\nW_{10} \\\\approx 244 \\\\times 0.904382 \\\\approx 220.5\\n\\\\]\\n\\nRounding to the nearest whole number, Cal O\'Ree can expect to weigh approximately \\\\( 221 \\\\) pounds at the end of the ten weeks.",\n    answer=\'221\'\n)\nNext, let\'s set up an evaluator for the zero-shot module above, before prompt Since we want strong reasoning, we\'ll use the large GPT-4o as the teacher model (used to bootstrap reasoning for the small LM at optimization time) but not as the prompt model (used to craft instructions) or the task model (trained).\nGPT-4o will be invoked only a small number of times. The model involved directly in optimization and in the resulting (optimized) program will be GPT-4o-mini.\nWe will also specify\nmax_bootstrapped_demos=4\nwhich means we want at most four bootstrapped examples in the prompt and\nmax_labeled_demos=4\nwhich means that, in total between bootstrapped and pre-labeled examples, we want at most four.\nIn\xa0[\xa0]:\nCopied!\nkwargs\n=\ndict\n(\nnum_threads\n=\nTHREADS\n,\nteacher_settings\n=\ndict\n(\nlm\n=\ngpt4o\n),\nprompt_model\n=\ngpt4o_mini\n)\noptimizer\n=\ndspy\n.\nMIPROv2\n(\nmetric\n=\ndataset\n.\nmetric\n,\nauto\n=\n"medium"\n,\n**\nkwargs\n)\nkwargs\n=\ndict\n(\nrequires_permission_to_run\n=\nFalse\n,\nmax_bootstrapped_demos\n=\n4\n,\nmax_labeled_demos\n=\n4\n)\noptimized_module\n=\noptimizer\n.\ncompile\n(\nmodule\n,\ntrainset\n=\ndataset\n.\ntrain\n,\n**\nkwargs\n)\nkwargs = dict(num_threads=THREADS, teacher_settings=dict(lm=gpt4o), prompt_model=gpt4o_mini)\noptimizer = dspy.MIPROv2(metric=dataset.metric, auto="medium", **kwargs)\n\nkwargs = dict(requires_permission_to_run=False, max_bootstrapped_demos=4, max_labeled_demos=4)\noptimized_module = optimizer.compile(module, trainset=dataset.train, **kwargs)\nIn\xa0[7]:\nCopied!\nevaluate\n(\noptimized_module\n)\nevaluate(optimized_module)\nAverage Metric: 310.00 / 350 (88.6%): 100%|██████████| 350/350 [01:31<00:00,  3.84it/s]\n2024/11/28 18:59:19 INFO dspy.evaluate.evaluate: Average Metric: 310 / 350 (88.6%)\nquestion\nexample_reasoning\nexample_answer\npred_reasoning\npred_answer\nmethod\n0\nWhat is the smallest integer value of $c$ such that the function $...\nThe given function has a domain of all real numbers if and only if...\n1\nThe function \\( f(x) = \\frac{x^2 + 1}{x^2 - It was pretty straightforward to improve quality from 74% to over 88% on a held-out set here.\nThat said, for reasoning tasks like this, you will often want to consider more advanced strategies, like:\nA\ndspy.ReAct\nmodule with access to a calculator function or\ndspy.PythonInterpreter\nEnsembling multiple optimized prompts with a majority vote (or an Aggregator module) on top\nJust to understand what changed, let\'s view the prompt after optimization. Alternatively, if you enabled MLflow tracing following above instructions, you can compare the prompts before and after optimization in the rich trace UI.\nIn\xa0[8]:\nCopied!\ndspy\n.\ninspect_history\n()\ndspy.inspect_history()\n[2024-11-28T18:59:19.176586]\nSystem message:\nYour input fields are:\n1. `question` (str)\n\nYour output fields are:\n1. `reasoning` (str)\n2. `answer` (str)\n\nAll interactions will be structured in the following way, with the appropriate values filled in.\n\n[[ ## question ## ]]\n{question}\n\n[[ ## reasoning ## ]]\n{reasoning}\n\n[[ ## answer ## ]]\n{answer}\n\n[[ ## completed ## ]]\n\nIn adhering to this structure, your objective is: \n        Analyze the `question` provided, and systematically apply mathematical reasoning to derive the `answer`. Ensure to articulate each step of your thought process in the `reasoning` field, detailing how you identify relationships and formulate equations to arrive at the solution.\nUser message:\n[[ ## question ## ]]\nIf $|4x+2|=10$ and $x<0$, what is the value of $x$?\n\nRespond with the corresponding output fields, starting with the field `[[ ## reasoning ## ]]`, then `[[ ## answer ## ]]`, and then ending with the marker for `[[ ## completed ## ]]`.\nAssistant message:\n[[ ## reasoning ## ]]\nThe equation $|4x+2|=10$ implies two possible equations: $4x+2=10$ or $4x+2=-10$. We solve each equation separately.\n\n1. For $4x+2=10$:\n   \\[\n   4x + 2 = 10 \\\\\n   4x = 8 \\\\\n   x = 2\n   \\]\n   However, $x=2$ does not satisfy the condition $x<0$.\n\n2. For $4x+2=-10$:\n   \\[\n   4x + 2 = -10 \\\\\n   4x = -12 \\\\\n   x = -3\n   \\]\n   The solution $x = -3$ satisfies the condition $x<0$.\n\nTherefore, the value of $x$ is $\\boxed{-3}$.\n\n[[ ## answer ## ]]\n-3\n\n[[ ## completed ## ]]\nUser message:\n[[ ## question ## ]]\nGiven that $-4$ is a solution to $x^2 + bx -36 = 0$, what is the value of $b$?\n\nRespond with the corresponding output fields, starting with the field `[[ ## reasoning ## ]]`, then `[[ ## answer ## ]]`, and then ending with the marker for `[[ ## completed ## ]]`.\nAssistant message:\n[[ ## reasoning ## ]]\nThe product of the roots of this quadratic is $-36/1=-36$, so the other solution must be $-36/-4=9$. That means that the sum of the solutions is $-4+9=5$. The sum of the solutions is also $-b/1=-b$. Thus, $-b=5$ and $b=\\boxed{-5}$.\n\n[[ ## answer ## ]]\n-5\n\n[[ ## completed ## ]]\nUser message:\n[[ ## question ## ]]\nRichard is building a rectangular playground from 200 feet of fencing. The fencing must entirely enclose the playground. What is the maximum area of this playground?\n\nRespond with the corresponding output fields, starting with the field `[[ ## reasoning ## ]]`, then `[[ ## answer ## ]]`, and then ending with the marker for `[[ ## completed ## ]]`.\nAssistant message:\n[[ ## reasoning ## ]]\nLet the length of the playground be $l$ and the width be $w$. We have the equation $2l+2w=200 \\Rightarrow l + w = 100$. We want to maximize the area of this rectangular playground, which is given by $lw$. From our equation, we know that $l=100-w$. Substituting this in to our expression for area, we have \\[(100-w)(w)=100w-w^2\\]We will now complete the square to find the maximum value of this expression. Factoring a $-1$ out, we have \\[-(w^2-100w)\\]In order for the expression inside the parenthesis to be a perfect square, we need to add and subtract $(100/2)^2=2500$ inside the parenthesis. Doing this, we get \\[-(w^2-100w+2500-2500) \\Rightarrow -(w-50)^2+2500\\]Since the maximum value of $-(w-50)^2$ is 0 (perfect squares are always nonnegative), the maximum value of the entire expression is 2500, which is achieved when $w=50$ and $l=100-w=50$ (the playground is a square). Thus, the maximum area of the playground is $\\boxed{2500}$ square feet.\n\n[[ ## answer ## ]]\n2500\n\n[[ ## completed ## ]]\nUser message:\n[[ ## question ## ]]\nAlice and Bob are playing a game. Alice starts first. On Alice\'s turn, she flips a coin. If she gets a heads, she wins. If not, it becomes Bob\'s turn. On Bob\'s turn, he flips a coin. If he gets a tails, he wins. If not, it becomes Alice\'s turn. What is the probability that Alice wins the game?\n\nRespond with the corresponding output fields, starting with the field `[[ ## reasoning ## ]]`, then `[[ ## answer ## ]]`, and then ending with the marker for `[[ ## completed ## ]]`.\nAssistant message:\n[[ ## reasoning ## ]]\nAlice has a $1/2$ chance of winning the game on her first turn. If she doesn\'t, then the probability that she wins the game on her second turn is $1/8,$ since she must not win on her first flip ($1/2$ chance), Bob must not win on his first flip ($1/2$ chance), and then Alice must win on her second flip ($1/2$ chance). The probability that she wins the game on her third turn is $1/32,$ and in general, the probability that she wins the game on her $k^\\text{th}$ turn is $(1/2)^{2k-1}.$ Thus, the probability that Alice wins is an infinite geometric series with first term $1/2$ and common ratio $1/4.$ So, the probability that Alice wins the game is $$\\frac{\\frac12}{1-\\frac14} = \\boxed{\\frac{2}{3}}.$$OR\n\nNote that the only difference between the odds of Alice or Bob winning is who goes first. Because Bob goes second, the odds of him winning on his $k^\\text{th}$ flip is half of the odds that Alice wins on her $k^\\text{th}$ flip, since Alice must first get a tails before Bob gets a chance to win. Thus, if $a$ is Alice\'s chance of winning, and $b$ is Bob\'s chance of winning, then $a = 2b.$ Also, since someone must win, $a + b = 1.$ It follows that $a = 2/3$ and $b = 1/3,$ so Alice has a $\\boxed{\\frac{2}{3}}$ chance of winning the game.\n\n[[ ## answer ## ]]\n\\frac{2}{3}\n\n[[ ## completed ## ]]\nUser message:\n[[ ## question ## ]]\nFind the smallest possible value of $$\\frac{(y-x)^2}{(y-z)(z-x)} + \\frac{(z-y)^2}{(z-x)(x-y)} + \\frac{(x-z)^2}{(x-y)(y-z)},$$ where $x,y,$ and $z$ are distinct real numbers.\n\nRespond with the corresponding output fields, starting with the field `[[ ## reasoning ## ]]`, then `[[ ## answer ## ]]`, and then ending with the marker for `[[ ## completed ## ]]`.\nResponse:\n[[ ## reasoning ## ]]\nLet \\( S = \\frac{(y-x)^2}{(y-z)(z-x)} + \\frac{(z-y)^2}{(z-x)(x-y)} + \\frac{(x-z)^2}{(x-y)(y-z)} \\).\n\nTo find the minimum value of \\( S \\), we can use the method of symmetry and the Cauchy-Schwarz inequality. \n\nBy applying the Cauchy-Schwarz inequality, we have:\n\\[\n\\left( \\frac{(y-x)^2}{(y-z)(z-x)} + \\frac{(z-y)^2}{(z-x)(x-y)} + \\frac{(x-z)^2}{(x-y)(y-z)} \\right) \\left( (y-z)(z-x) + (z-x)(x-y) + (x-y)(y-z) \\right) \\geq \\left( (y-x) + (z-y) + (x-z) \\right)^2.\n\\]\nThe right-hand side simplifies to:\n\\[\n(y-x) + (z-y) + (x-z) = 0,\n\\]\nwhich means the right-hand side is \\( 0 \\). Therefore, we have:\n\\[\nS \\cdot \\left( (y-z)(z-x) + (z-x)(x-y) + (x-y)(y-z) \\right) \\geq 0.\n\\]\nThis implies that \\( S \\) is non-negative.\n\nNext, we can test specific values for \\( x, y, z \\) to find the minimum. Let\'s set \\( x = 0, y = 1, z = 2 \\):\n\\[\nS = \\frac{(1-0)^2}{(1-2)(2-0)} + \\frac{(2-1)^2}{(2-0)(0-1)} + \\frac{(0-2)^2}{(0-1)(1-2)}.\n\\]\nCalculating each term:\n1. The first term:\n   \\[\n   \\frac{1^2}{(-1)(2)} = \\frac{1}{-2} = -\\frac{1}{2}.\n   \\]\n2. The second term:\n   \\[\n   \\frac{1^2}{(2)(-1)} = \\frac{1}{-2} = -\\frac{1}{2}.\n   \\]\n3. The third term:\n   \\[\n   \\frac{(-2)^2}{(-1)(-1)} = \\frac{4}{1} = 4.\n   \\]\n\nThus,\n\\[\nS = -\\frac{1}{2} - \\frac{1}{2} + 4 = 3.\n\\]\n\nTo check if this is indeed the minimum, we can also consider the case when \\( x, y, z \\) are the roots of a quadratic polynomial. By symmetry and the nature of the expression, we can conclude that the minimum value of \\( S \\) is indeed \\( 3 \\).\n\nTherefore, the smallest possible value of \\( S \\) is \\( \\boxed{3} \\).\n\n[[ ## answer ## ]]\n3\n\n[[ ## completed ## ]]', 'last_updated': '2025-04-11T17:39:57.491562'}

task:\nIn\xa0[5]:\nCopied!\nexample\n=\ntrainset\n[\n0\n]\nprint\n(\n"Claim:"\n,\nexample\n.\nclaim\n)\nprint\n(\n"Pages that must be retrieved:"\n,\nexample\n.\ntitles\n)\nexample = trainset[0]\n\nprint("Claim:", example.claim)\nprint("Pages that must be retrieved:", example.titles)\nClaim: This director is known for his work on Miss Potter. The Academy of Motion Picture Arts and Sciences presents the award in which he was nominated for his work in "Babe".\nPages that must be retrieved: [\'Miss Potter\', \'Chris Noonan\', \'Academy Award for Best Director\']\nNow, let\'s define a function to do the search in Wikipedia. This will use our BM25 index.\nIn\xa0[6]:\nCopied!\ndef\nsearch\n(\nquery\n:\nstr\n,\nk\n:\nint\n)\n->\nlist\n[\nstr\n]:\ntokens\n=\nbm25s\n.\ntokenize\n(\nquery\n,\nstopwords\n=\n"en"\n,\nstemmer\n=\nstemmer\n,\nshow_progress\n=\nFalse\n)\nresults\n,\nscores\n=\nretriever\n.\nretrieve\n(\ntokens\n,\nk\n=\nk\n,\nn_threads\n=\n1\n,\nshow_progress\n=\nFalse\n)\nrun\n=\n{\ncorpus\n[\ndoc\n]:\nfloat\n(\nscore\n)\nfor\ndoc\n,\nscore\nin\nzip\n(\nresults\n[\n0\n],\nscores\n[\n0\n])}\nreturn\nrun\ndef search(query: str, k: int) -> list[str]:\n    tokens = bm25s.tokenize(query, stopwords="en", stemmer=stemmer, show_progress=False)\n    results, scores = retriever.retrieve(tokens, k=k, n_threads=1, show_progress=False)\n    run = {corpus[doc]: float(score) for doc, score in zip(results[0], scores[0])}\n    return run\nNow, let\'s define the multi-hop program in DSPy. It\'s going to be super simple: it\'ll take a\nclaim\nand produce a list\ntitles: list[str]\n.\nIt will do this via two sub-modules:\ngenerate_query\nand\nappend_notes\n.\nIn\xa0[7]:\nCopied!\nclass\nHop\n(\ndspy\n.\nModule\n):\ndef\n__init__\n(\nself\n,\nnum_docs\n=\n10\n,\nnum_hops\n=\n4\n):\nself\n.\nnum_docs\n,\nself\n.\nnum_hops\n=\nnum_docs\n,\nnum_hops\nself\n.\ngenerate_query\n=\ndspy\n.\nChainOfThought\n(\n\'claim, notes -> query\'\n)\nself\n.\nappend_notes\n=\ndspy\n.\nChainOfThought\n(\n\'claim, notes, context -> new_notes: list[str], titles: list[str]\'\n)\ndef\nforward\n(\nself\n,\nclaim\n:\nstr\n)\n->\nlist\n[\nstr\n]:\nnotes\n=\n[]\ntitles\n=\n[]\nfor\n_\nin\nrange\n(\nself\n.\nnum_hops\n):\nquery\n=\nself\n.\ngenerate_query\n(\nclaim\n=\nclaim\n,\nnotes\n=\nnotes\n)\n.\nquery\ncontext\n=\nsearch\n(\nquery\n,\nk\n=\nself\n.\nnum_docs\n)\nprediction\n=\nself\n.\nappend_notes\n(\nclaim\n=\nclaim\n,\nnotes\n=\nnotes\n,\ncontext\n=\ncontext\n)\nnotes\n.\nextend\n(\nprediction\n.\nnew_notes\n)\ntitles\n.\nextend\n(\nprediction\n.\ntitles\n)\nreturn\ndspy\n.\nPrediction\n(\nnotes\n=\nnotes\n,\ntitles\n=\nlist\n(\nset\n(\ntitles\n)))\nclass Hop(dspy.Module):\n    def __init__(self, num_docs=10, num_hops=4):\n        self.num_docs, self.num_hops = num_docs, num_hops\n        self.generate_query = dspy.ChainOfThought(\'claim, notes -> query\')\n        self.append_notes = dspy.ChainOfThought(\'claim, notes, context -> new_notes: list[str], titles: list[str]\')\n\n    def forward(self, claim: str) -> list[str]:\n        notes = []\n        titles = []\n\n        for _ in range(self.num_hops):\n            query = self.generate_query(claim=claim, notes=notes).query\n            context = search(query, k=self.num_docs)\n            prediction = self.append_notes(claim=claim, notes=notes, context=context)\n            notes.extend(prediction.new_notes)\n            titles.extend(prediction.titles)\n        \n        return dspy.Prediction(notes=notes, titles=list(set(titles)))\nGreat. Now let\'s set up an evaluation metric,\ntop5_recall\n.\nIt will return the fraction of the gold pages (which are always 3) that are retrieved in the top-5 titles returned by the program.\nIn\xa0[8]:\nCopied!\ndef\ntop5_recall\n(\nexample\n,\npred\n,\ntrace\n=\nNone\n):\ngold_titles\n=\nexample\n.\ntitles\nrecall\n=\nsum\n(\nx\nin\npred\n.\ntitles\n[:\n5\n]\nfor\nx\nin\ngold_titles\n)\n/\nlen\n(\ngold_titles\n)\n# If we\'re "bootstrapping" for optimization, return True if and only if the recall is perfect.\nif\ntrace\nis\nnot\nNone\n:\nreturn\nrecall\n>=\n1.0\n# If we\'re just doing inference, just measure the recall.\nreturn\nrecall\nevaluate\n=\ndspy\n.\nEvaluate\n(\ndevset\n=\ndevset\n,\nmetric\n=\ntop5_recall\n,\nnum_threads\n=\n16\n,\ndisplay_progress\n=\nTrue\n,\ndisplay_table\n=\n5\n)\ndef top5_recall(example, pred, trace=None):\n    gold_titles = example.titles\n    recall = sum(x in pred.titles[:5] for x in gold_titles) / len(gold_titles)\n\n    # If we\'re "bootstrapping" for optimization, return True if and only if the recall is perfect.\n    if trace is not None:\n        return recall >= 1.0\n    \n    # If we\'re just doing inference, just measure the recall.\n    return recall\n\nevaluate = dspy.Evaluate(devset=devset, metric=top5_recall, num_threads=16, display_progress=True, display_table=5)\nLet\'s evaluate our off-the-shelf program!\nIn\xa0[9]:\nCopied!\nevaluate\n(\nHop\n())\nevaluate(Hop())\nAverage Metric: 27.67 / 98 (28.2%):  32%|███▏      | 97/300 [00:02<00:04, 49.34it/s]\n2024/12/25 12:18:00 ERROR dspy.utils.parallelizer: Error processing item Example({\'claim\': "All That is the show that the co-creator with the host of Vibe and Wild \'N Out had a debut on.", \'titles\': [\'Chris Spencer (actor)\', \'Nick Cannon\', \'Vibe (talk show)\']}) (input_keys={\'claim\'}): Expected dict_keys([\'reasoning\', \'new_notes\', \'titles\']) but got dict_keys([\'reasoning\', \'new_notes\']). ...\nOut[9]:\n31.33\nTracking Evaluation Results in MLflow Experiment\nTo track and visualize the evaluation results over time, you can record the results in MLflow Experiment.\nimport\nmlflow\nwith\nmlflow\n.\nstart_run\n(\nrun_name\n=\n"hop_evaluation"\n):\nevaluate\n=\ndspy\n.\nEvaluate\n(\ndevset\n=\ndevset\n,\nmetric\n=\ntop5_recall\n,\nnum_threads\n=\n16\n,\ndisplay_progress\n=\nTrue\n,\n# {'url': 'https://dspy.ai/tutorials/output_refinement/best-of-n-and-refine/', 'title': 'Output Refinement - DSPy', 'structure': [{'level': 1, 'title': 'Output Refinement: BestOfN and Refine', 'content': 'BothBestOfNandRefineare DSPy modules designed to improve the reliability and quality of predictions by making multipleLMcalls with different parameter settings. Both modules stop when they have reachedNattempts or when thereward_fnreturns an award above thethreshold.'}, {'level': 2, 'title': 'BestOfN', 'content': 'BestOfNis a module that runs the provided module multiple times (up toN) with different temperature settings. It returns either the first prediction that passes a specified threshold or the one with the highest reward if none meets the threshold.'}, {'level': 3, 'title': 'Basic Usage', 'content': 'Lets say we wanted to have the best chance of getting a one word answer from the model. We could useBestOfNto try multiple temperature settings and return the best result.\n\nimportdspydefone_word_answer(args,pred:dspy.Prediction)->float:return1.0iflen(pred.answer.split())==1else0.0best_of_3=dspy.BestOfN(module=dspy.ChainOfThought("question -> answer"),N=3,reward_fn=one_word_answer,threshold=1.0)result=best_of_3(question="What is the capital of Belgium?")print(result.answer)# Brussels'}, {'level': 3, 'title': 'Error Handling', 'content': 'By default, if the module encounters an error during an attempt, it will continue trying until it reachesNattempts. You can adjust this behavior with thefail_countparameter:\n\nbest_of_3=dspy.BestOfN(module=qa,N=3,reward_fn=one_word_answer,threshold=1.0,fail_count=1)best_of_3(question="What is the capital of Belgium?")# raises an error after the first failure'}, {'level': 2, 'title': 'Refine', 'content': "Refineextends the functionality ofBestOfNby adding an automatic feedback loop. After each unsuccessful attempt (except the final one), it automatically generates detailed feedback about the module's performance and uses this feedback as hints for subsequent runs."}, {'level': 3, 'title': 'Basic Usage', 'content': 'importdspydefone_word_answer(args,pred:dspy.Prediction)->float:return1.0iflen(pred.answer.split())==1else0.0refine=dspy.Refine(module=dspy.ChainOfThought("question -> answer"),N=3,reward_fn=one_word_answer,threshold=1.0)result=refine(question="What is the capital of Belgium?")print(result.answer)# Brussels'}, {'level': 3, 'title': 'Error Handling', 'content': 'LikeBestOfN,Refinewill try up toNtimes by default, even if errors occur. You can control this with thefail_countparameter:\n\n# Stop after the first errorrefine=dspy.Refine(module=qa,N=3,reward_fn=one_word_answer,threshold=1.0,fail_count=1)'}, {'level': 2, 'title': 'Comparison: BestOfN vs. Refine', 'content': "Both modules serve similar purposes but differ in their approach:\n\nBestOfNsimply tries different temperature settings and selects the best resulting prediction as defined by thereward_fn.Refineadds an feedback loop, using the lm to generate a detailed feedback about the module's own performance using the previous prediction and the code in thereward_fn. This feedback is then used as hints for subsequent runs."}, {'level': 2, 'title': 'Practical Examples', 'content': ''}, {'level': 3, 'title': 'Ensuring Factual Correctness', 'content': 'importdspyclassFactualityJudge(dspy.Signature):"""Determine if a statement is factually accurate."""statement:str=dspy.InputField()is_factual:bool=dspy.OutputField()factuality_judge=dspy.ChainOfThought(FactualityJudge)deffactuality_reward(args,pred:dspy.Prediction)->float:statement=pred.answerresult=factuality_judge(statement)return1.0ifresult.is_factualelse0.0refined_qa=dspy.Refine(module=dspy.ChainOfThought("question -> answer"),N=3,reward_fn=factuality_reward,threshold=1.0)result=refined_qa(question="Tell me about Belgium\'s capital city.")print(result.answer)'}, {'level': 3, 'title': 'Summarization - Controlling Response Length', 'content': 'importdspydefideal_length_reward(args,pred:dspy.Prediction)->float:"""Reward the summary for being close to 75 words with a tapering off for longer summaries."""word_count=len(pred.summary.split())distance=abs(word_count-75)returnmax(0.0,1.0-(distance/125))optimized_summarizer=dspy.BestOfN(module=dspy.ChainOfThought("text -> summary"),N=50,reward_fn=ideal_length_reward,threshold=0.9)result=optimized_summarizer(text="[Long text to summarize...]")print(result.summary)'}, {'level': 2, 'title': 'Migration fromdspy.Suggestanddspy.Assert', 'content': 'BestOfNandRefineare the replacements fordspy.Suggestanddspy.Assertas of DSPy 2.6.'}], 'full_text': 'Output Refinement: BestOfN and Refine\nBoth\nBestOfN\nand\nRefine\nare DSPy modules designed to improve the reliability and quality of predictions by making multiple\nLM\ncalls with different parameter settings. Both modules stop when they have reached\nN\nattempts or when the\nreward_fn\nreturns an award above the\nthreshold\n.\nBestOfN\nBestOfN\nis a module that runs the provided module multiple times (up to\nN\n) with different temperature settings. It returns either the first prediction that passes a specified threshold or the one with the highest reward if none meets the threshold.\nBasic Usage\nLets say we wanted to have the best chance of getting a one word answer from the model. We could use\nBestOfN\nto try multiple temperature settings and return the best result.\nimport\ndspy\ndef\none_word_answer\n(\nargs\n,\npred\n:\ndspy\n.\nPrediction\n)\n->\nfloat\n:\nreturn\n1.0\nif\nlen\n(\npred\n.\nanswer\n.\nsplit\n())\n==\n1\nelse\n0.0\nbest_of_3\n=\ndspy\n.\nBestOfN\n(\nmodule\n=\ndspy\n.\nChainOfThought\n(\n"question -> answer"\n),\nN\n=\n3\n,\nreward_fn\n=\none_word_answer\n,\nthreshold\n=\n1.0\n)\nresult\n=\nbest_of_3\n(\nquestion\n=\n"What is the capital of Belgium?"\n)\nprint\n(\nresult\n.\nanswer\n)\n# Brussels\nError Handling\nBy default, if the module encounters an error during an attempt, it will continue trying until it reaches\nN\nattempts. You can adjust this behavior with the\nfail_count\nparameter:\nbest_of_3\n=\ndspy\n.\nBestOfN\n(\nmodule\n=\nqa\n,\nN\n=\n3\n,\nreward_fn\n=\none_word_answer\n,\nthreshold\n=\n1.0\n,\nfail_count\n=\n1\n)\nbest_of_3\n(\nquestion\n=\n"What is the capital of Belgium?"\n)\n# raises an error after the first failure\nRefine\nRefine\nextends the functionality of\nBestOfN\nby adding an automatic feedback loop. After each unsuccessful attempt (except the final one), it automatically generates detailed feedback about the module\'s performance and uses this feedback as hints for subsequent runs.\nBasic Usage\nimport\ndspy\ndef\none_word_answer\n(\nargs\n,\npred\n:\ndspy\n.\nPrediction\n)\n->\nfloat\n:\nreturn\n1.0\nif\nlen\n(\npred\n.\nanswer\n.\nsplit\n())\n==\n1\nelse\n0.0\nrefine\n=\ndspy\n.\nRefine\n(\nmodule\n=\ndspy\n.\nChainOfThought\n(\n"question -> answer"\n),\nN\n=\n3\n,\nreward_fn\n=\none_word_answer\n,\nthreshold\n=\n1.0\n)\nresult\n=\nrefine\n(\nquestion\n=\n"What is the capital of Belgium?"\n)\nprint\n(\nresult\n.\nanswer\n)\n# Brussels\nError Handling\nLike\nBestOfN\n,\nRefine\nwill try up to\nN\ntimes by default, even if errors occur. You can control this with the\nfail_count\nparameter:\n# Stop after the first error\nrefine\n=\ndspy\n.\nRefine\n(\nmodule\n=\nqa\n,\nN\n=\n3\n,\nreward_fn\n=\none_word_answer\n,\nthreshold\n=\n1.0\n,\nfail_count\n=\n1\n)\nComparison: BestOfN vs. Refine\nBoth modules serve similar purposes but differ in their approach:\nBestOfN\nsimply tries different temperature settings and selects the best resulting prediction as defined by the\nreward_fn\n.\nRefine\nadds an feedback loop, using the lm to generate a detailed feedback about the module\'s own performance using the previous prediction and the code in the\nreward_fn\n. This feedback is then used as hints for subsequent runs.\nPractical Examples\nEnsuring Factual Correctness\nimport\ndspy\nclass\nFactualityJudge\n(\ndspy\n.\nSignature\n):\n"""Determine if a statement is factually accurate."""\nstatement\n:\nstr\n=\ndspy\n.\nInputField\n()\nis_factual\n:\nbool\n=\ndspy\n.\nOutputField\n()\nfactuality_judge\n=\ndspy\n.\nChainOfThought\n(\nFactualityJudge\n)\ndef\nfactuality_reward\n(\nargs\n,\npred\n:\ndspy\n.\nPrediction\n)\n->\nfloat\n:\nstatement\n=\npred\n.\nanswer\nresult\n=\nfactuality_judge\n(\nstatement\n)\nreturn\n1.0\nif\nresult\n.\nis_factual\nelse\n0.0\nrefined_qa\n=\ndspy\n.\nRefine\n(\nmodule\n=\ndspy\n.\nChainOfThought\n(\n"question -> answer"\n),\nN\n=\n3\n,\nreward_fn\n=\nfactuality_reward\n,\nthreshold\n=\n1.0\n)\nresult\n=\nrefined_qa\n(\nquestion\n=\n"Tell me about Belgium\'s capital city."\n)\nprint\n(\nresult\n.\nanswer\n)\nSummarization - Controlling Response Length\nimport\ndspy\ndef\nideal_length_reward\n(\nargs\n,\npred\n:\ndspy\n.\nPrediction\n)\n->\nfloat\n:\n"""\nReward the summary for being close to 75 words with a tapering off for longer summaries.\n"""\nword_count\n=\nlen\n(\npred\n.\nsummary\n.\nsplit\n())\ndistance\n=\nabs\n(\nword_count\n-\n75\n)\nreturn\nmax\n(\n0.0\n,\n1.0\n-\n(\ndistance\n/\n125\n))\noptimized_summarizer\n=\ndspy\n.\nBestOfN\n(\nmodule\n=\ndspy\n.\nChainOfThought\n(\n"text -> summary"\n),\nN\n=\n50\n,\nreward_fn\n=\nideal_length_reward\n,\nthreshold\n=\n0.9\n)\nresult\n=\noptimized_summarizer\n(\ntext\n=\n"[Long text to summarize...]"\n)\nprint\n(\nresult\n.\nsummary\n)\nMigration from\ndspy.Suggest\nand\ndspy.Assert\nBestOfN\nand\nRefine\nare the replacements for\ndspy.Suggest\nand\ndspy.Assert\nas of DSPy 2.6.', 'last_updated': '2025-04-11T17:41:56.223854'}

Specifically, let\'s build\na system for answering Tech questions\n, e.g. about Linux or iPhone apps.\nInstall the latest DSPy via\npip install -U dspy\nand follow along. If you\'re looking instead for a conceptual overview of DSPy, this\nrecent lecture\nis a good place to start.\nConfiguring the DSPy environment.\n¶\nLet\'s tell DSPy that we will use OpenAI\'s\ngpt-4o-mini\nin our modules. To authenticate, DSPy will look into your\nOPENAI_API_KEY\n. You can easily swap this out for\nother providers or local models\n.\nRecommended: Set up MLflow Tracing to understand what\'s happening under the hood.\nMLflow DSPy Integration\n¶\nMLflow\nis an LLMOps tool that natively integrates with DSPy and offer explainability and experiment tracking. In this tutorial, you can use MLflow to visualize prompts and optimization progress as traces to understand the DSPy\'s behavior better. They provide great visibility into the model\'s behavior and helps you understand the DSPy\'s concepts better throughout the tutorial.\nTo kearn more about the integration, visit\nMLflow DSPy Documentation\nas well.\nIn\xa0[1]:\nCopied!\nimport\ndspy\nlm\n=\ndspy\n.\nLM\n(\n\'openai/gpt-4o-mini\'\n)\ndspy\n.\nconfigure\n(\nlm\n=\nlm\n)\nimport dspy\n\nlm = dspy.LM(\'openai/gpt-4o-mini\')\ndspy.configure(lm=lm)\nExploring some basic DSPy Modules.\n¶\nYou can always prompt the LM directly via\nlm(prompt="prompt")\nor\nlm(messages=[...])\n. However, DSPy gives you\nModules\nas a better way to define your LM functions.\nThe simplest module is\ndspy.Predict\n. It takes a\nDSPy Signature\n, i.e. a structured input/output schema, and gives you back a callable function for the behavior you specified. Let\'s use the "in-line" notation for signatures to declare a module that takes a\nquestion\n(of type\nstr\n) as input and produces a\nresponse\nas an output.\nIn\xa0[2]:\nCopied!\nqa\n=\ndspy\n.\nPredict\n(\n\'question: str -> response: str\'\n)\nresponse\n=\nqa\n(\nquestion\n=\n"what are high memory and low memory on linux?"\n)\nprint\n(\nresponse\n.\nresponse\n)\nqa = dspy.Predict(\'question: str -> response: str\')\nresponse = qa(question="what are high memory and low memory on linux?")\n\nprint(response.response)\nIn Linux, "high memory" and "low memory" refer to different regions of the system\'s memory address space, particularly in the context of 32-bit architectures.\n\n- **Low Memory**: This typically refers to the memory that is directly accessible by the kernel. In a 32-bit system, this is usually the first 896 MB of RAM (from 0 to 896 MB). The kernel can directly map this memory, making it faster for the kernel to access and manage. Low memory is used for kernel data structures and for user processes that require direct access to memory.\n\n- **High Memory**: This refers to the memory above the low memory limit, which is not directly accessible by the kernel in a 32-bit system. This area is typically above 896 MB. The kernel cannot directly access this memory without using special mechanisms, such as mapping it into the kernel\'s address space when needed. High memory is used for user processes that require more memory than what is available in low memory.\n\nIn summary, low memory is directly accessible by the kernel, while high memory requires additional steps for the kernel to access it, especially in 32-bit systems. In 64-bit systems, this distinction is less significant as the kernel can address a much larger memory space directly.\nNotice how the variable names we specified in the signature defined our input and output argument names and their role.\nNow, what did DSPy do to build this\nqa\nmodule? Nothing fancy in this example, yet. The module passed your signature, LM, and inputs to an Adapter, which is a layer that handles structuring the inputs and parsing structured outputs to fit your signature.\nLet\'s see it directly. You can inspect the\nn\nlast prompts sent by DSPy easily. Alternatively, if you enabled MLflow Tracing above, you can see the full LLM interactions for each program execution in a tree view.\nIn\xa0[3]:\nCopied!\ndspy\n.\ninspect_history\n(\nn\n=\n1\n)\ndspy.inspect_history(n=1)\n[2024-11-23T23:16:35.966534]\nSystem message:\nYour input fields are:\n1. `question` (str)\n\nYour output fields are:\n1. `response` (str)\n\nAll interactions will be structured in the following way, with the appropriate values filled in.\n\n[[ ## question ## ]]\n{question}\n\n[[ ## response ## ]]\n{response}\n\n[[ ## completed ## ]]\n\nIn adhering to this structure, your objective is: \n        Given the fields `question`, produce the fields `response`.\nUser message:\n[[ ## question ## ]]\nwhat are high memory and low memory on linux?\n\nRespond with the corresponding output fields, starting with the field `[[ ## response ## ]]`, and then ending with the marker for `[[ ## completed ## ]]`.\nResponse:\n[[ ## response ## ]]\nIn Linux, "high memory" and "low memory" refer to different regions of the system\'s memory address space, particularly in the context of 32-bit architectures.\n\n- **Low Memory**: This typically refers to the memory that is directly accessible by the kernel. In a 32-bit system, this is usually the first 896 MB of RAM (from 0 to 896 MB). The kernel can directly map this memory, making it faster for the kernel to access and manage. Low memory is used for kernel data structures and for user processes that require direct access to memory.\n\n- **High Memory**: This refers to the memory above the low memory limit, which is not directly accessible by the kernel in a 32-bit system. This area is typically above 896 MB. The kernel cannot directly access this memory without using special mechanisms, such as mapping it into the kernel\'s address space when needed. High memory is used for user processes that require more memory than what is available in low memory.\n\nIn summary, low memory is directly accessible by the kernel, while high memory requires additional steps for the kernel to access it, especially in 32-bit systems. In 64-bit systems, this distinction is less significant as the kernel can address a much larger memory space directly.\n\n[[ ## completed ## ]]\nDSPy has various built-in modules, e.g.\ndspy.ChainOfThought\n,\ndspy.ProgramOfThought\n, and\ndspy.ReAct\n. These are interchangeable with basic\ndspy.Predict\n: they take your signature, which is specific to your task, and they apply general-purpose prompting techniques and inference-time strategies to it.\nFor example,\ndspy.ChainOfThought\nis an easy way to elicit\nreasoning\nout of your LM before it commits to the outputs requested in your signature.\nIn the example below, we\'ll omit\nstr\ntypes (as the default type is string). You should feel free to experiment with other fields and types, e.g. try\ntopics: list[str]\nor\nis_realistic: bool\n.\nIn\xa0[4]:\nCopied!\ncot\n=\ndspy\n.\nChainOfThought\n(\n\'question -> response\'\n)\ncot\n(\nquestion\n=\n"should curly braces appear on their own line?"\n)\ncot = dspy.ChainOfThought(\'question -> response\')\ncot(question="should curly braces appear on their own line?")\nOut[4]:\nPrediction(\n    reasoning=\'The placement of curly braces on their own line depends on the coding style and conventions being followed. In some programming languages and style guides, such as the Allman style, curly braces are placed on their own line to enhance readability. In contrast, other styles, like K&R style, place the opening brace on the same line as the control statement. Ultimately, it is a matter of personal or team preference, and consistency within a project is key.\',\n    response=\'Curly braces can appear on their own line depending on the coding style you are following. If you prefer a style that enhances readability, such as the Allman style, then yes, they should be on their own line. However, if you are following a different style, like K&R, they may not need to be. Consistency is important, so choose a style and stick with it.\'\n)\nInterestingly, asking for reasoning can make the output\nresponse\nshorter in this case. Is this a good thing or a bad thing? It depends on what you need: there\'s no free lunch, but DSPy gives you the tools to experiment with different strategies extremely quickly.\nBy the way,\ndspy.ChainOfThought\nis implemented in DSPy, using\ndspy.Predict\n. This is a good place to\ndspy.inspect_history\nif you\'re curious.\nUsing DSPy well involves evaluation and iterative development.\n¶\nYou already know a lot about DSPy at this point. If all you want is quick scripting, this much of DSPy already enables a lot. Sprinkling DSPy signatures and modules into your Python control flow is a pretty ergonomic way to just get stuff done with LMs.\nThat said, you\'re likely here because you want to build a high-quality system and improve it over time. The way to do that in DSPy is to iterate fast by evaluating the quality of your system and using DSPy\'s powerful tools, e.g. Optimizers.\nManipulating Examples in DSPy.\n¶\nTo measure the quality of your DSPy system, you need (1) a bunch of input values, like\nquestion\ns for example, and (2) a\nmetric\nthat can score the quality of an output from your system. Metrics vary widely. Some metrics need ground-truth labels of ideal outputs, e.g. for classification or question answering. Other metrics are self-supervised, e.g. checking faithfulness or lack of hallucination, perhaps using a DSPy program as a judge of these qualities.\nLet\'s load a dataset of questions and their (pretty long) gold answers. Since we started this notebook with the goal of building\na system for answering Tech questions\n, we obtained a bunch of StackExchange-based questions and their correct answers from the\nRAG-QA Arena\ndataset.\nIn\xa0[5]:\nCopied!\nimport\nujson\nfrom\ndspy.utils\nimport\ndownload\n# Download question--answer pairs from the RAG-QA Arena "Tech" dataset.\ndownload\n(\n"https://huggingface.co/dspy/cache/resolve/main/ragqa_arena_tech_examples.jsonl"\n)\nwith\nopen\n(\n"ragqa_arena_tech_examples.jsonl"\n)\nas\nf\n:\ndata\n=\n[\nujson\n.\nloads\n(\nline\n)\nfor\nline\nin\nf\n]\nimport ujson\nfrom dspy.utils import download\n\n# Download question--answer pairs from the RAG-QA Arena "Tech" dataset.\ndownload("https://huggingface.co/dspy/cache/resolve/main/ragqa_arena_tech_examples.jsonl")\n\nwith open("ragqa_arena_tech_examples.jsonl") as f:\n    data = [ujson.loads(line) for line in f]\nIn\xa0[6]:\nCopied!\n# Inspect one datapoint.\ndata\n[\n0\n]\n# Inspect one datapoint.\ndata[0]\nOut[6]:\n{\'question\': \'why igp is used in mpls?\',\n \'response\': "An IGP exchanges routing prefixes between gateways/routers.  \\nWithout a routing protocol, you\'d have to configure each route on every router and you\'d have no dynamic updates when routes change because of link failures. \\nFuthermore, within an MPLS network, an IGP is vital for advertising the internal topology and ensuring connectivity for MP-BGP inside the network.",\n \'gold_doc_ids\': [2822, 2823]}\nGiven a simple dict like this, let\'s create a list of\ndspy.Example\ns, which is the datatype that carries training (or test) datapoints in DSPy.\nWhen you build a\ndspy.Example\n, you should generally specify\n.with_inputs("field1", "field2", ...)\nto indicate which fields are inputs. The other fields are treated as labels or metadata.\nIn\xa0[7]:\nCopied!\ndata\n=\n[\ndspy\n.\nExample\n(\n**\nd\n)\n.\nwith_inputs\n(\n\'question\'\n)\nfor\nd\nin\ndata\n]\n# Let\'s pick an `example` here from the data.\nexample\n=\ndata\n[\n2\n]\nexample\ndata = [dspy.Example(**d).with_inputs(\'question\') for d in data]\n\n# Let\'s pick an `example` here from the data.\nexample = data[2]\nexample\nOut[7]:\nExample({\'question\': \'why are my text messages coming up as maybe?\', \'response\': \'This is part of the Proactivity features new with iOS 9: It looks at info in emails to see if anyone with this number sent you an email and if it finds the phone number associated with a contact from your email, it will show you "Maybe". \\n\\nHowever, it has been suggested there is a bug in iOS 11.2 that can result in "Maybe" being displayed even when "Find Contacts in Other Apps" is disabled.\', \'gold_doc_ids\': [3956, 3957, 8034]}) (input_keys={\'question\'})\nNow, let\'s divide the data into:\nTraining (and with it Validation) set:\nThese are the splits you typically give to DSPy optimizers.\nOptimizers typically learn directly from the training examples and check their progress using the validation examples.\nIt\'s good to have 30--300 examples for training and validation each.\nFor prompt optimizers in particular, it\'s often better to pass\nmore\nvalidation than training.\nBelow, we\'ll use 200 in total.
